content:
10 min read
Just now

--

Part 2 of “Self-Hosting LLMs in Production” — taking the model you validated in Part 1 and putting it behind your own secure, public endpoint.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Introduction
By the end of this article, you’ll have your own self-hosted LLM model with an OpenAI-compatible API: https://your-domain.com/v1Answering with an open-source model, on a GPU you rent by the hour, behind TLS and an API key. Any OpenAI SDK works against it — just swap the base_url.
The stack is vLLM in Docker Compose, route-guarded by an API Key, Nginx in front, Let’s Encrypt for TLS, and a firewall that allows exactly the necessary ports.
This is Part 2 of “Self-Hosting LLMs in Production.” Part 1 covered the requirement gathering, i.e budget, VRAM math, and validating the model on OpenRouter before renting anything.
If you skipped it, the only numbers you need from there are:
1. GPU used: 48 GB L40S Scaleway GPU
2. Model Used: Ministral-3–14B-Instruct-2512 (based on part 1, I changed from reasoning to instruct model for demonstration because the reasoning model requires more output tokens and might fail in the case of max model length 1024)
3. Max-model length: 1024
What you’ll need(checklist)

• Scaleway account with GPU quota (note: L40S may require a quota request; for me, the turnaround time was 2–3 hours).

• A domain (or subdomain) you control — needed for TLS

• Hugging Face account + access token (note whether your chosen model is gated)

• Your numbers from Part 1: max-model-len, expected concurrency, VRAM budget.
Step 1: Create a Hugging Face account and get an access token

• Once you have created an account, you will be able to create an access token for downloading the model from the profile menu (top right)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

2. Once you create a credential (use fine-grained read-only), store it temporarily somewhere safe.
Step 2: Creating a GPU instance and doing the necessary security setups for hosting the LLM model

• Before creating a GPU, you can configure your SSH key in Scaleway via the Scaleway UI SSH section. This is a one-time setup, and it will ensure that every time you start a new instance, your SSH keys are automatically added to the instance's authorized keys. (Note: Scaleway controls the authorized keys from its own config, so whenever you start and stop an instance, the SSH keys defined in the instance's authorized keys are overwritten)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How to create a SSH KEY in scaleway UI Config2. Now go into the Compute section in the Scaleway app and follow the steps on the screen to create a new GPU instance.
3. Once the instance is created and you have logged into the instance (in your terminal) using SSH, we will do some basic security changes and a few sanity checks to ensure everything is secured and works:
1. Create a new Linux user. We will use this user instead of the root user. Run the below command inside the root user on the instance.
# adds a new user (here user is called ritwik), follow the steps provided to add name, contact info, password
adduser ritwik
# add sudo priveleges to the user
usermod -aG sudo ritwik2. Allow your local system to SSH using the new user. Run the below command inside the instance root user.
# copy your SSH key over
mkdir -p /home/ritwik/.ssh
cp /root/.ssh/authorized_keys /home/ritwik/.ssh/
chown -R ritwik:ritwik /home/ritwik/.ssh
chmod 700 /home/ritwik/.ssh
chmod 600 /home/ritwik/.ssh/authorized_keysOnce this is done, as a sanity check, open a new terminal and try logging into the instance using ssh <user-name>@<instance-ip>. The instance IP can be found in the Scaleway app. If you can log in and you see the username in the terminal, then this step is done.
3. Prevent SSH into root-user: Now, as the next step, we will prevent anyone from logging into the instance as root-user. Run the below command as root-user in the terminal
cat > /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/99-hardening.conf << 'EOF'
PermitRootLogin no
PasswordAuthentication no
KbdInteractiveAuthentication no
PubkeyAuthentication yes
MaxAuthTries 3
EOFsshd -t # validate syntax: do not skip
systemctl restart ssh
Once done, try logging in as the root user in a new terminal. If you see permission denied, the root user has been locked out from outside.
Now close the terminal and login as new the user created.
4. Check if NVIDIA drivers are installed: run the command below. You will see the driver's details. If you don't see that, it means NVIDIA drivers are not installed (This shouldn't happen on Scaleway as its instances have preinstalled drivers)
nvidia-smiIf you need a dedicated tutorial on how to install a new driver on any Ubuntu instance, please comment. I will create that as well.
5. Check if Docker is installed: run the command below to check if Docker is installed
docker --version
docker compose versionStep 2: Creating a Docker Compose file and installing the model on your GPU instance
1. Let's create a directory where the Dockerfile, .env, and downloaded model weight will live

mkdir -p ~/ministral/models
cd ~/ministral2. Create an .env file: We will access this model api using an api key(this is a security layer), so our env file will contain an hf_token and vllm-api key
# create a vllm api key
VLLM_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 32)
# use the below command to print vllm api key(save it) we will use it later to test api endpoint
echo "Your API key (save this): $VLLM_KEY" # create environment file with the api keys and hf_token
cat > .env << EOF
HF_TOKEN=hf_your_actual_token_here
VLLM_API_KEY=$VLLM_KEY
EOF
chmod 600 .env
# verify our .env file
cat .env
3. Create the Docker Compose file: Use the Docker Compose file below
cat > docker-compose.yml << 'EOF'
services:
ministral:
image: vllm/vllm-openai:latest
container_name: ministral-14b-bf16
restart: unless-stopped
network_mode: host
env_file:
- .env
environment:
- HF_HOME=/root/.cache/huggingface
- HF_TOKEN=${HF_TOKEN}
- HUGGING_FACE_HUB_TOKEN=${HF_TOKEN}
- VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=FLASH_ATTN
- PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True
volumes:
- ./models:/root/.cache/huggingface
deploy:
resources:
reservations:
devices:
- driver: nvidia
count: 1
capabilities: [gpu]
command: >
--model mistralai/Ministral-3-14B-Instruct-2512-BF16
--tokenizer-mode mistral
--config-format mistral
--load-format mistral
--host 127.0.0.1
--port 8000
--max-model-len 1024
--max-num-batched-tokens 8192
--gpu-memory-utilization 0.9
--api-key ${VLLM_API_KEY}
healthcheck:
test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8000/health"]
interval: 30s
timeout: 15s
retries: 5
start_period: 300s
EOFSide Note: Below is a short explanation of Dockerfile contents
services:
ministral: # service name, used in compose commands (logs, exec, etc.)
image: vllm/vllm-openai:latest # official vLLM server image with OpenAI-compatible API
container_name: ministral-14b-bf16 # fixed container name instead of an auto-generated one
restart: unless-stopped # auto-restart on crash and on boot, unless manually stopped
network_mode: host # container shares the host's network stack directly (no port mapping)
env_file:
- .env # loads HF_TOKEN and VLLM_API_KEY from the .env file
environment:
- HF_HOME=/root/.cache/huggingface # where HuggingFace stores models *inside* the container
- HF_TOKEN=${HF_TOKEN} # auth token for downloading the model from HuggingFace
- HUGGING_FACE_HUB_TOKEN=${HF_TOKEN} # same token under its older env var name (both are checked)
- VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=FLASH_ATTN # use FlashAttention kernels (faster, less memory)
- PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True # reduces GPU memory fragmentation
volumes:
- ./models:/root/.cache/huggingface # persist model weights on host so restarts don't re-download
deploy:
resources:
reservations:
devices:
- driver: nvidia # request GPU access via nvidia-container-toolkit
count: 1 # give the container 1 GPU
capabilities: [gpu] # GPU compute capability
command: > # arguments passed to the vLLM server on startup
--model mistralai/Ministral-3-14B-Instruct-2512-BF16 # HuggingFace repo to download and serve
--tokenizer-mode mistral # use Mistral's native tokenizer (mistral-common)
--config-format mistral # read model config in Mistral's own format
--load-format mistral # load weights from Mistral's consolidated file format
--host 127.0.0.1 # listen on loopback only — reachable via nginx, not the internet
--port 8000 # API port
--max-model-len 1024 # max tokens per request (prompt + output combined)
--max-num-batched-tokens 8192 # max tokens processed per GPU forward pass (throughput knob)
--gpu-memory-utilization 0.9 # let vLLM use 90% of VRAM (weights + KV cache)
--api-key ${VLLM_API_KEY} # require this Bearer token on all /v1 endpoints
healthcheck:
test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8000/health"] # probe the health endpoint
interval: 30s # check every 30 seconds
timeout: 15s # fail the probe if no response in 15s
retries: 5 # mark unhealthy after 5 consecutive failures
start_period: 300s # grace period for model download/load before checks count4. Run the Dockerfile and watch the logs for the model to start
# start the docker service, running this command for first time will download necessary libraries so it will take sometime
sudo docker compose up -d # check the logs
sudo docker compose logs -f
Once you see the lines below in the logs, you know your model has started
ministral-14b-bf16 | (APIServer pid=1) INFO: Started server process [1]
ministral-14b-bf16 | (APIServer pid=1) INFO: Waiting for application startup.
ministral-14b-bf16 | (APIServer pid=1) INFO: Application startup complete.Note: If you don't see this and you see some errors, please let me know in the comments. I will help you debug.
5. Run some tests to check if the model is serving output. We will use the curl command.
# loads VLLM_API_KEY into your shell for testing
source .envcurl http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-H "Authorization: Bearer $VLLM_API_KEY" \
-d '{
"model": "mistralai/Ministral-3-14B-Instruct-2512-BF16",
"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello, who are you?"}],
"max_tokens": 100
}'
Step 3: Exposing the api to the public with Nginx + TLS + Let's Encrypt
Now our model works on the instance GPU; we need to expose it securely to public api access.

• Go into your domain provider and add an “A-record” entry, and the destination should be the instance IP. To verify if the domain redirect is successful, use the command below in a terminal window.
ping <your_domain_name>If you see response packets from your GPU IP, the domain redirect was successful.
2. Install nginx + certbot: run the command below
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y nginx certbot python3-certbot-nginx3. Generate the gateway key: we will use a gateway key at the Nginx level to thus not expose the vllm api key to the public api. Run the command below
openssl rand -hex 324. Set up an nginx config
# creates a new config file
sudo nano /etc/nginx/sites-available/ministral.configAdd the below config in nginx ministral.config

map_hash_bucket_size 128;
map $http_authorization $gateway_auth_ok {
default 0;
"Bearer <gateway-key-from-step-3>" 1;
}server {
listen 80;
server_name ministral.yourdomain.tld;
location /v1/ {
# gateway auth check
if ($gateway_auth_ok = 0) {
return 401 '{"error":"invalid or missing API key"}';
}
proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8000;
proxy_http_version 1.1;
proxy_set_header Host $host;
proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
# swap the client's gateway key for the internal vLLM key
proxy_set_header Authorization "Bearer <your-VLLM_API_KEY-value>";
proxy_read_timeout 600s; # long generations
proxy_buffering off; # required for streaming responses
client_max_body_size 10m;
}
location = /health {
proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8000/health; # unauthenticated, for uptime monitoring
}
location / {
return 404; # nothing else exposed (including /metrics)
}
}
Run the command to test if the config i
